query: fever non-hemolytic transfusion reaction risk factors

retrieved_docs.title: Noninfectious Complications of Blood Transfusion -- Issues of Concern -- Febrile nonhemolytic transfusion reaction
retrieved_docs.content: Cytokines that accumulate during storage may also play a pivotal role as the transfusion reaction risk increases with the time a unit of blood product remains stored. Clinicians should stop the transfusion immediately and administer antipyretics like acetaminophen if necessary. The patient should undergo evaluation for other causes of fever, like sepsis and an acute hemolytic transfusion reaction. Necessary lab evaluation may include blood and urine cultures and the laboratory tests discussed later in this section (Refer to " Acute hemolytic transfusion reaction " for more information on the necessary lab tests).

retrieved_docs.title: Noninfectious Complications of Blood Transfusion -- Issues of Concern -- Febrile nonhemolytic transfusion reaction
retrieved_docs.content: Febrile nonhemolytic transfusion reactions (FNHTRs) are one of the most commonly encountered transfusion reactions. [9] Generally associated with RBC and platelet transfusions, the symptoms are most often mild and self-limiting; however, the symptoms overlap with other serious transfusion reactions, making FNHTR a diagnosis of exclusion. Patients with FNHTR have a temperature greater than 100.4 °F (38 °C) and a change of at least 1.8 °F (1.0 °C) from their pretransfusion temperature during or within 4 hours of the transfusion. Occasionally, patients may present with no fever and only chills and rigors. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Noninfectious Complications of Blood Transfusion -- Issues of Concern -- Delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction
retrieved_docs.content: The most common routes of prior exposure are pregnancy and prior blood transfusions. [22] The RBC antigens most commonly involved are those of the Kidd or Rh system. [23] According to the United States Centers for Disease Control and Prevention Biovigilance Network, to establish the diagnosis of a DHTR an affected patient must exhibit a positive direct DAT between 24 hours and 28 days after the transfusion, identification of the RBC antibody in the serum, and laboratory findings such as inappropriate hemoglobin increment, spherocytes on peripheral blood smear, or findings of hemolysis like low-grade fever, jaundice, increased LDH, increased indirect bilirubin, or decreased haptoglobin.

retrieved_docs.title: Noninfectious Complications of Blood Transfusion -- Issues of Concern -- Nonimmune hemolysis
retrieved_docs.content: Nonimmune hemolysis is a rare complication of blood product transfusion potentially caused by thermal, osmotic, or mechanical injury to RBCs; the result is hemoglobinemia and hemoglobinuria without significant clinical symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: Noninfectious Complications of Blood Transfusion -- Issues of Concern -- Febrile nonhemolytic transfusion reaction
retrieved_docs.content: Meperidine can be helpful for severe rigors. Leukoreduction is a helpful technique to reduce the incidence of FNHTR. [11] Premedication with acetaminophen or an antihistamine does not help reduce the risk of FNHTR. [12] However, their benefit in patients with a previous FNHTR is unclear.

retrieved_docs.title: Noninfectious Complications of Blood Transfusion -- Clinical Significance -- Febrile nonhemolytic transfusion reactions
retrieved_docs.content: FNHTRs are one of the most common transfusion reactions and occur in approximately 1% of all transfusions. [14] Clinicians diagnose FNHTR after excluding other causes of fever like sepsis and other transfusion reactions. FHNTRs are benign and cause no longterm harm. Treatment is symptomatic with antipyretics and meperidine for rigors when necessary. Leukoreduction helps prevent future FNHTRs. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Transfusion Reactions -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Fever and/or chills are most commonly associated with a febrile, non-hemolytic reaction, however; they can also be the first sign of a more serious acute hemolytic reaction, TRALI, or septic transfusion reaction. If the temperature rises 1 C or higher from the temperature at the start of transfusion, the transfusion should be stopped. Acute hemolytic reaction or bacterial contamination should be suspected if there is a greater rise in temperature, or more serious symptoms (e.g., rigors). Respiratory Distress/Dyspnea

retrieved_docs.title: Blood Transfusion -- Complications -- Adverse Event and Approximate Risk Per Unit Transfusion of RBC
retrieved_docs.content: Febrile reaction: 1:60 Transfusion-associated circulatory overload: 1:100 Allergic reaction: 1:250 TRALI: 1:12,000 Hepatitis C infection: 1:1,149,000 Human immunodeficiency virus infection: 1:1,467,000 Fatal hemolysis: 1:1,972,000 Febrile Reactions [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Postoperative Fever -- History and Physical -- Immediate Fever
retrieved_docs.content: Febrile non-hemolytic transfusion reaction: Fevers, chills, and malaise 1 to 6 hours after surgery (without hemolysis). Management: Stop transfusion (rule out hemolytic transfusion reaction) and give antipyretics (avoid aspirin in the thrombocytopenic patient).

retrieved_docs.title: Noninfectious Complications of Blood Transfusion -- Issues of Concern -- Febrile nonhemolytic transfusion reaction
retrieved_docs.content: Experts believe the underlying pathophysiology is due to cytokines released by white blood cells in stored blood products that have not been leukoreduced. [10] Donor leukocytes and recipient antibodies lead to interleukin (IL)-1 release from donor leukocytes or recipient monocytes. IL-1 produces fever by stimulating prostaglandin E2 production in the hypothalamus. IL-6, IL-8, and tumor necrosis factor-α are additional implicated cytokines.

retrieved_docs.title: Infectious Complications of Blood Transfusion -- Issues of Concern -- Bacterial Transfusion-Transmitted Infections
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnosis for transfusion pyrexia - defined as an increase in temperature greater than one degree Celsius from baseline or greater than 38 degrees Celsius with or without rigors/ chills occurring in the blood recipient of a unit of blood or blood component without other explanation – is broad and includes the following: Febrile non Hemolytic transfusion reaction Hemolytic transfusion reactions (Immediate and Delayed) Transfusion transmitted malaria Transfusion-related acute lung injury (TRALI) Transfusion-associated graft versus host disease (TAGVHD)

retrieved_docs.title: Platelet Transfusion -- Complications -- Febrile Nonhemolytic Transfusion Reaction
retrieved_docs.content: Management includes stopping the transfusion, obtaining lab tests to rule out a hemolytic transfusion reaction, evaluating other fever causes, and administering antipyretics and meperidine or pethidine for chills and rigors. [22] Premedications aiming at preventing febrile nonhemolytic transfusion reactions have shown no benefit. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Noninfectious Complications of Blood Transfusion -- Clinical Significance -- Specific Complications and Their Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Although RBCs are the most common blood component transfused, platelets account for the most reported reactions. Platelet transfusions are associated with a high frequency of febrile and anaphylactoid reactions. Clinicians must rapidly differentiate between life-threatening and non life-threatening cases when evaluating transfusion reactions. Potentially fatal TACO, TRALI, AHTRs, anaphylaxis, and hyperhemolysis syndrome require immediate medical intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: Massive Transfusion -- Complications -- Febrile non-hemolytic transfusion reaction
retrieved_docs.content: Febrile non-hemolytic transfusion reactions (FNHTR) occur due to the release of cytokines from WBCs in the transfused product. Fever is the sole symptom in this type of reaction. [39] [40] FNHTRs occur in 10% to 15% of patients who receive blood products, and the clinical judgment of a healthcare professional guides the decision as to whether to continue or halt the transfusion.

retrieved_docs.title: Infectious Complications of Blood Transfusion -- Issues of Concern -- Bacterial Transfusion-Transmitted Infections
retrieved_docs.content: The clinician often requires a high index of suspicion to suspect the transfusion as the culprit of a fever. It should not be overlooked during the evaluation of pyrexia, especially after a reasonable interval from the transfusion has elapsed. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Noninfectious Complications of Blood Transfusion -- Issues of Concern -- Acute hemolytic transfusion reaction
retrieved_docs.content: In the presence of AHTR, the clinician should stop the transfusion immediately, and the patient should receive an intravenous infusion of normal saline to maintain a urine output of 1 mL/kg/hr and monitor renal function. Supportive measures such as supplemental oxygen may be necessary. When clinicians suspect AHTR, the following laboratory tests are necessary:

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 955
retrieved_docs.content: Arthritis, acquired hemolytic anemia, cold‑agglutinin hemolytic anemia, coronary artery inflammation, and a form of hemolytic anemia that is not caused by autoimmunity are all classified within the “Immune disorders/Autoimmune (SMQ)” grouping. This categorization reflects that these conditions are relevant to immune‑mediated disease processes and are monitored together in pharmacovigilance and clinical practice. While most of the listed disorders are driven by autoimmune mechanisms, the non‑autoimmune hemolytic anemia is included in the same SMQ to ensure comprehensive safety surveillance of all hemolytic events that may arise in the context of immune system modulation. Recognizing the shared immunological context of these entities aids clinicians in identifying potential autoimmune contributions, guiding diagnostic evaluation, and informing therapeutic decisions across a spectrum of inflammatory and hematologic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1239
retrieved_docs.content: The collection “اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ)” comprises a range of hematologic and inflammatory conditions that share an autoimmune basis. Aplastic anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل) is considered an autoimmune disorder, as are autoimmune hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي الناجم عن المناعة الذاتية) and cold‑type hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد). Arthritis (التهاب المفاصل) and coronary artery inflammation (التهاب الشريان التاجي) are also members of this autoimmune group, illustrating the systemic nature of immune‑mediated disease. Although non‑immune hemolytic anemia (فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية) is listed among these entities, it does not result from autoantibody‑mediated red‑cell destruction; its inclusion reflects clinical overlap and underscores the importance of distinguishing immune from non‑immune mechanisms when evaluating hemolysis. Recognizing that these diverse conditions are linked by an underlying autoimmune process helps clinicians diagnose, monitor, and manage patients with immune‑driven pathology more effectively.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 4 -- Community 2900
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is recognized as an etiological descriptor for several medical conditions, including iron‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الحديد), transfusion reaction (تفاعل نقل الدم), mortality (التموت), and acute yellow liver congestion (ضُمورُ الكَبِد الأصفر الحادّ). By serving as an allowed etiological qualifier, it helps clinicians attribute these conditions to specific underlying causes, thereby supporting accurate diagnosis and appropriate patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2818
retrieved_docs.content: Blood transfusion, referred to in Arabic as نقل الدم, is classified as نقل الدم and is recognized as a potential cause of complications associated with surgical procedures or other medical care. Awareness of this relationship is important for minimizing transfusion‑related risks and improving patient safety during operative and clinical interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 713
retrieved_docs.content: The category “اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ)” encompasses a range of hematologic and inflammatory conditions, including فقر الدم اللا تنسجي (aplastic anemia), فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب (acquired hemolytic anemia), فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية (hemolytic anemia not caused by autoimmunity), التهاب المفاصل (arthritis), and التهاب الشريان التاجي (coronary artery inflammation). These entities are grouped together because they involve immune system dysregulation, whether through autoantibody‑mediated attacks, cell‑mediated immune injury, or broader immune‑related inflammatory processes. While most of the listed disorders are driven by autoimmune mechanisms, the non‑autoimmune hemolytic anemia is included in the classification for organizational purposes despite its different pathogenesis. Recognizing that these diverse diseases share an underlying immune component helps clinicians identify common diagnostic pathways and consider immunomodulatory therapies across different organ systems, thereby improving patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 0 -- Community 17
retrieved_docs.content: growth restriction, Rh‑mediated hemolytic disease, diverse neonatal hemorrhagic and jaundice disorders, perinatal infections, and birth trauma are interrelated, underscoring the need for integrated assessment, prevention of delivery trauma and infection, and specialized care for extremely preterm infants to improve outcomes across the fetal, neonatal, and early infant periods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2276
retrieved_docs.content: The category of autoimmune disorders (اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية) encompasses several distinct conditions, including التهاب المفاصل (arthritis), فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب (acquired hemolytic anemia), فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد (cold‑agglutinin hemolytic anemia), and التهاب الشريان التاجي (coronary artery inflammation). Each of these diseases is characterized by an inappropriate immune response that targets the body’s own tissues, leading to joint inflammation, destruction of red blood cells, or inflammation of the coronary arteries. Recognizing that these diverse clinical entities share a common autoimmune basis aids clinicians in diagnosing, monitoring, and applying immunomodulatory therapies across this spectrum of immune‑mediated illnesses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 1079
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease of the newborn is a complex condition that includes several related neonatal complications. It is linked to neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation, which results from the delayed development of the newborn’s conjugating system. The condition also encompasses specific child manifestations such as نزيف حديثي الولادة (neonatal hemorrhage), نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ (neonatal respiratory tract hemorrhage), يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد (neonatal jaundice caused by hemolysis in the fetus or newborn), and يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان (neonatal jaundice associated with preterm birth). Recognizing these interrelated entities is essential for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive management of affected newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 919
retrieved_docs.content: Arthritis, autoimmune hemolytic anemia, cold‑type hemolytic anemia, non‑immune hemolytic anemia, adrenal insufficiency, and coronary arteritis are all classified within the MedDRA Standardized MedDRA Query for immune disorders/autoimmune conditions (اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية SMQ). This grouping reflects that the majority of these disorders—arthritis, autoimmune hemolytic anemia, cold‑type hemolytic anemia, adrenal insufficiency, and coronary arteritis—are driven by dysregulated immune mechanisms, such as autoantibody production or inflammatory immune cell activity. Non‑immune hemolytic anemia, although not caused by an autoimmune process, is included in the same SMQ to ensure comprehensive pharmacovigilance and safety monitoring for any hemolytic events that may arise in the context of immune‑modulating therapies. Recognizing these conditions as members of the autoimmune SMQ aids clinicians and researchers in identifying patterns of immune‑related pathology, assessing drug safety signals, and guiding patient management strategies that address underlying immune dysfunction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 421
retrieved_docs.content: Blood transfusion, known in Arabic as نقل الدم, is a medical procedure used to replace lost blood components and is classified under the broader concept of blood replacement therapy. It encompasses specific subtypes, including نقل الدم الذاتي (autologous blood transfusion), which involves collecting and reinfusing the patient’s own blood. Although blood transfusion is a valuable therapeutic intervention, it can also be a cause of complications related to surgical procedures or other medical care, underscoring the importance of careful indication, thorough screening, and vigilant monitoring to minimize adverse outcomes. Recognizing the hierarchical relationship between general blood transfusion, its child category autologous transfusion, and the potential for procedural complications is essential for safe and effective patient management.

Overall, the interrelationships among chronic kidney disease–related anemia, various deficiency and hemolytic anemias, thalassemia, and related immune‑mediated conditions underscore the importance of integrated diagnostic and therapeutic strategies that address both hematologic abnormalities and their associated systemic manifestations.

Laboratory evaluation, particularly measurement of total hemoglobin concentration, is interpreted against these interrelated conditions to guide diagnosis, risk stratification, and therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2041
retrieved_docs.content: Aplastic anemia, non‑regenerative anemia, arthritis, hemolytic anemia that is not caused by autoimmunity, and coronary artery inflammation are all classified within the broader category of immune disorders/autoimmune conditions. This grouping reflects the shared underlying involvement of the immune system in the pathogenesis of these diverse diseases, whether through direct autoimmune attack on blood‑forming tissues, inflammatory processes affecting joints, or vascular inflammation of the coronary arteries. Recognizing these entities as members of the immune/autoimmune disorder spectrum helps clinicians consider common immunologic mechanisms, guide diagnostic evaluation, and select therapeutic strategies that target immune dysregulation across these seemingly disparate clinical presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 150
retrieved_docs.content: (يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان); and * Jaundice specifically caused by Rh alloimmunization in the fetus or newborn (يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد). These jaundice subtypes are often evaluated together with total hemoglobin concentration, where values below the reference range support the diagnosis of hemolytic disease. Recognizing the interrelated nature of neonatal infections, genetic syndromes, hemolytic processes, diverse jaundice mechanisms and hemorrhagic events—along with appropriate laboratory interpretation—is essential for accurate diagnosis, timely intervention and optimal care of affected newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2327
retrieved_docs.content: The category of autoimmune disorders (اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ)) includes a range of hematologic and inflammatory conditions. Aplastic anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل) is classified as an autoimmune disease, as is non‑regenerative anemia (فقر الدم اللا تنسجي) and acquired hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب). Inflammatory joint disease (التهاب المفاصل) and coronary artery inflammation (التهاب الشريان التاجي) are also considered members of this autoimmune group. Recognizing that these diverse diseases share an underlying autoimmune mechanism guides clinicians toward appropriate immunomodulatory treatments and vigilant monitoring for systemic involvement.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2812
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia, which is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, is a hereditary hemoglobin disorder that can precipitate serious respiratory complications in adults. Clinically, داء الكريات المنجلية is associated with respiratory failure, respiratory insufficiency, and adult cardiac arrest, underscoring the need for vigilant respiratory monitoring and prompt intervention in patients affected by this disease.
